flashcards.front: What is the scope resolution operator in C++?
flashcards.back: The scope resolution operator (::) is a unary operator used to access entities in different scopes, allowing precise control over name visibility and namespace management in C++.

flashcards.front: How do you access a global variable when a local variable has the same name?
flashcards.back: Use the :: operator before the global variable name. For example: ::globalVar accesses the global variable when a local variable named globalVar also exists.

flashcards.front: How is :: used to define a member function outside the class definition?
flashcards.back: Use ClassName::functionName() syntax. For example: void MyClass::display() { } defines the display function belonging to MyClass outside the class.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between :: and . (dot) operator?
flashcards.back: :: accesses entities by scope name (::globalVar, std::cout) while . accesses members through an object instance (obj.member). :: is used for static members and scope names; . is for object members.
:: is used to access namespace members without using 'using' directive. Example: std::cout accesses cout from std namespace; ::name accesses global namespace members.

flashcards.front: How is :: used with enum class in C++?
flashcards.back: Enum class constants require scope resolution. Example: enum class Color {Red, Green}; Color c = Color::Red; // Must use :: to access enum values.

flashcards.front: How do you access base class members using :: in inheritance?
flashcards.back: Use BaseClass::memberName to access base class members from derived class, especially when derived class overrides them. Example: Derived::display() can call Base::display().

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of :: with static class members?
flashcards.back: :: is used to define static member variables outside the class and to access static members without an object. Example: int MyClass::count = 0; MyClass::accessStatic().

flashcards.front: How is :: used to access nested classes?
flashcards.back: Use OuterClass::InnerClass to reference nested classes. Example: OuterClass::InnerClass obj; creates an object of the nested class.

flashcards.front: What does the leading :: operator do at the beginning of an identifier?
flashcards.back: The leading :: refers to the global namespace, ensuring access to global variables or functions even when hidden by local or class-scope declarations.

flashcards.front: How is :: used with inline member functions outside class?
flashcards.back: Same as regular outside definitions: inline void MyClass::func() { } uses MyClass:: to associate the function with the class scope.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between using std:: and 'using namespace std'?
flashcards.back: std:: explicitly accesses individual names from std namespace (:: used), while 'using namespace std' brings all names into current scope (potential name conflicts).

flashcards.front: How does :: help in disambiguation when header files conflict?
flashcards.back: By qualifying names with their namespace/class scope, :: eliminates ambiguity. Example: MyLib::String vs std::String removes confusion between different String definitions.

flashcards.front: Can :: be used with class templates?
flashcards.back: Yes, use ClassTemplate<T>::member to access members of template classes. Example: typename std::vector<T>::iterator in template code.

flashcards.front: What is the scope resolution operator's role in operator overloading?
flashcards.back: When overloading operators as member functions outside the class, use returnType ClassName::operator+(params). The :: associates the function with the class scope.
